evidence_citations.excerpt: I keep setting impossible standards for myself and end up not starting at all.
I think it’s the idea that if I don’t look perfect, people will think I’m not a good designer.
I worry that if I'm not perfect, people will think I'm not a good designer.

transcript.text: The wedding invite just landed, and I'm already nervous about how I look. My weight's gone up and I keep comparing myself to people, so I doubt I can pull together a good design piece. I keep setting impossible standards for myself and end up not starting at all.

The wedding invite can be really daunting, especially when you're already feeling self-conscious about your weight. It sounds like you're putting a lot of pressure on yourself to look a certain way, and that's making it hard for you to start on your design piece. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about your appearance at the wedding?

I keep imagining people staring at my clothes, judging me for not fitting the 'expected' body. I feel like I need to be flawless in every way, which stops me from even starting the project. The thought of being judged at the wedding makes me anxious and stuck.

The pressure to look a certain way at the wedding is really getting to you, and it's like these expectations are suffocating your creativity. You're imagining everyone's eyes on you, judging your clothes, your weight, and it feels like you need to be perfect to avoid that judgment. It's a heavy burden to carry, and it's stopping you from starting your project. Yeah, it makes sense that you're feeling stuck. Can you think of a time when you didn't let these expectations hold you back?

I can't think of a time when I didn't let the fear of being judged hold me back. Every time I try to sketch, the image of people staring at me feels like a wall. It just feels like my work will never be good enough if I look the way I do.

The fear of being judged is feeling like a constant companion, always lurking in the background and stopping you from creating. It's like the image of people staring at you is a wall that you just can't get past. You've been trying to sketch, but that fear is holding you back, making you feel like your work will never be good enough if you look the way you do. Can you tell me more about what's underlying this need to be flawless - is it the fear of being judged, or is there something else at play?

I think it’s the idea that if I don’t look perfect, people will think I’m not a good designer. That pressure feels like a wall I can’t get past. It makes me question whether I’m even worthy of the work I love.

The idea that your appearance defines your worth as a designer is a really painful one to carry. It's like you're feeling trapped by this need to present yourself in a certain way, and it's stopping you from doing what you love. Can you tell me more about what's driving this fear that people will judge your design skills based on how you look?

I’m scared that if I don’t look perfect, they’ll think my work is bad. I keep thinking my body is a judgment point, and that makes me doubt my skills. It’s like every design I create is filtered through that image.

It's really tough to hear that you're feeling like your body is a judgment point, and that it's making you doubt your skills as a designer. I can see how that would be a really painful and scary feeling, especially when you're already feeling self-conscious about your weight. It sounds like you're feeling trapped by this need to present yourself in a certain way, and it's stopping you from doing what you love. I want you to know that I'm here to support you, and that your worth and skills as a designer are not defined by your appearance. Can you think of any times when you've felt confident in your design skills, despite what you looked like?

I can’t think of a time when I felt truly confident in my design work. The last thing I did was a logo for a local bakery, and I still felt like it was all about how I look, not the work itself. It’s hard to separate my body image from the project and I keep setting impossible standards for myself.

You're really struggling to separate your body image from your design work, and it's like these two things are deeply connected for you. You mentioned that even when you did a logo for a local bakery, you still felt like it was all about how you look, not the work itself. It sounds like you're setting impossible standards for yourself, and that's making it hard for you to feel confident in your design skills. Can you tell me more about what you think would need to happen for you to feel more confident in your abilities as a designer, regardless of how you look?

Maybe if I could get honest, constructive feedback from a few trusted peers, I could see that my work has value independent of how I look. I'd also need to break my projects into smaller steps so I don't feel like I have to get everything perfect on the first try. That might help me feel less anxious about the wedding and my design.

Getting honest feedback from trusted peers and breaking down your projects into smaller steps could be a game-changer for you, helping you see your work's value beyond how you look. It might also help you feel less anxious about the wedding and your design. At the same time, I can imagine that taking this step might feel daunting, and you might be worried about how others will react. What do you think is the most important thing for you to consider as you weigh the potential benefits and drawbacks of seeking feedback and restructuring your approach?

I could reach out for feedback, but I keep thinking they'll judge me because of my weight. Splitting the work into smaller steps sounds good, yet I'm still terrified that a rough draft will make me look like a failure. I worry that if I'm not perfect, people will think I'm not a good designer. The whole thing just feels like another test of how I look.

You're feeling really anxious about reaching out for feedback because you're scared that people will judge you based on your weight, and that's making you doubt your design skills. It's like the fear of not being perfect is holding you back from taking that step. Can you tell me more about what's driving this fear that your weight will define your worth as a designer?

I guess it comes from always linking my worth to how I look, so when I imagine people judging me, I feel my design skills are judged too. If I don’t look the way they expect, it feels like I’m not competent, and that pushes me to hide my work. That pressure to be flawless in appearance and work makes any mistake feel like a personal failure.

You're feeling like your worth is tied to your appearance, and that's making you feel like any mistake in your design work is a personal failure. It's like you're under a microscope, and every flaw is magnified. I can see how that would be really scary and overwhelming. It sounds like you're trying to meet these impossible standards, and that's taking a toll on your confidence. Can you think of any ways you've managed to separate your self-worth from your appearance in the past, even if it was just for a moment?

The only time I felt a little less stuck was when a client called after a portfolio review and said my layout was strong, but even that felt like a fluke. I still worry that my body will ruin that praise. I want to find a real moment where I can separate my work from how I look.

You had a moment where a client praised your layout after a portfolio review, and that felt like a glimmer of hope, but even that praise was overshadowed by your fear that your body would somehow ruin it. It's like you're waiting for a moment where you can finally separate your work from how you look, and that's a really tough thing to wait for. Can you tell me more about what you think would need to happen for you to feel like you've truly separated your work from your appearance?

common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

stage_specific_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
stage_specific_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
stage_specific_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

stage_specific_candidates.label: Closed Question
stage_specific_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
stage_specific_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

stage_specific_candidates.label: Structure
stage_specific_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
stage_specific_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend, and the user needs me to reflect their emotions right now, so I'll prioritize empathy and understanding. Given the user's preparation stage for their body image concerns, I'll use the facilitate strategy to help them explore their thoughts and feelings. The evidence shows that the user has struggled to separate their body image from their design work, and they're terrified of being judged due to their weight. A specific moment that stands out is when a client praised their layout after a portfolio review, but even that felt like a fluke to the user. My entry point will be to acknowledge the user's fear of being judged and their desire to find a moment where they can separate their work from their appearance.

evidence_used.content: User is struggling with body image concerns, feeling like their weight and appearance are a judgment point
I guess it comes from always linking my worth to how I look, so when I imagine people judging me, I feel my design skills are judged too.
The user wants to find a real moment where they can separate their work from how they look

response.final_response: You had a moment where a client praised your layout after a portfolio review, and that felt like a glimmer of hope, but even that praise was overshadowed by your fear that your body would somehow ruin it. It's like you're waiting for a moment where you can finally separate your work from how you look, and that's a really tough thing to wait for. Can you tell me more about what you think would need to happen for you to feel like you've truly separated your work from your appearance?
